summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his job title of 'evangelist' and argues that the religious connotations are actually fitting for software development, which he sees as inherently religious in its tribal debates and passionate advocacy. He contends that effective evangelism requires both education and entertainment, and that the core skill underlying it—clear communication—is what separates great programmers from merely tolerable ones. Atwood suggests that many developers are already evangelists without realizing it, as anyone who shares their technical excitement and convinces others of their ideas is practicing evangelism. The post frames evangelism not as proselytization but as the essential skill of communicating ideas effectively.
key_points: The religious connotations of 'evangelist' are appropriate because software development itself functions like a religion with its holy wars and tribal loyalties
Good evangelism requires both education and entertainment—without humor, you're just a sanctimonious bore
Communication skills are what separate great programmers from tolerable ones, more than technical knowledge
Many developers are already evangelists without the title, simply by effectively sharing their enthusiasm and convincing others
Writing code alone isn't enough—you have to make people believe in your code's value
money_quotes: Software development is a religion, and any programmer worth his or her salt is the scarred veteran of a thousand religious wars.
Entertainment is a crucial part of being an evangelist. Without it, you're a sanctimonious bore.

The difference between a tolerable programmer and a great programmer is not how many programming languages they know, and it's not whether they prefer Python or Java. It's whether they can communicate their ideas.
It's questionable how effective you can be by writing code alone. You have to convince us how awesome your code is. You have to make us believe in the glory of that code.
Evangelism is simply sharing your excitement and enthusiasm with other people in an effective way.
We're all disciples of the church of Von Neumann. Praise the lord and pass the ones and zeroes!
Laughing is the only sane, rational response to any kind of evangelism – it means you have a sense of humor.
key_insight: Being a great programmer isn't just about writing code—it's about convincing others of your ideas, which makes communication and evangelism inseparable from technical excellence.
